skill.name: slb
skill.description: Simultaneous Launch Button adds peer review, command classification, and audit trails before AI agents run risky shell commands.
skill.summary: Require peer approval before AI agents execute destructive commands.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: The static scanner correctly identified high-risk command, network, filesystem, and environment themes, but many duplicate detections are documentation examples. No malicious prompt injection was found, but the skill intentionally installs and runs a command-execution guardrail with inherited credentials, hook writes, webhooks, and optional network daemon mode. Human marketplace review is recommended before publication.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped To Shell
high_findings.description: The installation instructions pipe a remote GitHub script directly into bash. This creates supply-chain risk because users execute remote code before inspection.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The curl-to-bash pattern is explicit on line 41 and is semantically an installer command. This is a well-known high-risk supply-chain pattern even if the project intent is legitimate.

high_findings.title: Arbitrary Command Execution In User Environment
high_findings.description: The skill is designed to run reviewed shell commands in the user shell. Those commands inherit cloud, Kubernetes, SSH, and database credentials.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation states commands run in the user shell and lists sensitive inherited environment values. Multiple command paths, including emergency execution, confirm this is core behavior.

medium_findings.title: Claude Hook Configuration Writes
medium_findings.description: The skill can generate Claude hook configuration into a hidden home-directory file. Hook changes can affect future agent command handling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation shows hook installation and writing to ~/.claude/hooks.json. This appears intentional, but it changes trusted agent integration state.

medium_findings.title: Webhook Notifications May Expose Command Metadata
medium_findings.description: The configuration supports webhook notifications. Pending command details, outcomes, or operational metadata could be sent to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: A webhook URL setting and environment variable are documented. The exact payload is not shown in SKILL.md, so exposure risk depends on implementation and configuration.

medium_findings.title: Optional TCP Daemon Exposure
medium_findings.description: The daemon can listen on 0.0.0.0 for Docker or remote agents. Authentication is documented, but network exposure increases attack surface.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sample configuration explicitly binds to 0.0.0.0 and allows IP ranges. Auth is enabled in the example, which lowers but does not remove the risk.

low_findings.title: Destructive Commands Mostly Appear As Classification Examples
low_findings.description: Many static detections for rm, database deletion, Kubernetes deletion, and force pushes are documentation examples used to classify risk tiers.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding sections describe risk-tier examples and UI samples, not hidden execution logic. These are still sensitive examples, but most duplicate static alerts are false positives.

low_findings.title: Cryptographic Weakness Alerts Are SHA-256 Documentation
low_findings.description: Static weak-crypto alerts map to SHA-256 command binding and hash acknowledgment documentation, not to MD5, SHA-1, or custom crypto.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections specifically reference SHA-256 and HMAC for integrity checks. No evidence of weak cryptographic algorithms was found in SKILL.md.

dangerous_patterns.title: Curl To Bash Installer
dangerous_patterns.description: Remote installation uses curl piped into bash, which executes fetched content immediately.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell pipeline is explicit in the installation section and directly matches the dangerous pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Emergency Execution Bypass
dangerous_patterns.description: Emergency execution can bypass normal peer review when a human provides a reason and hash acknowledgment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The bypass is intentionally documented and includes safeguards. It remains a dangerous pattern because it can execute destructive commands outside normal approval flow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent Hook Modification
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill can install or generate Claude Code hook configuration that controls whether commands are allowed, asked, or blocked.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documented hook integration changes command-handling behavior for Claude Code. That makes the pattern security-sensitive even if it supports the skill purpose.

content.value_statement: AI coding agents can execute destructive commands before a human notices. SLB adds peer review, risk classification, and audit logging before sensitive commands run.
actual_capabilities: Classifies shell commands into safe, caution, dangerous, and critical tiers.
Requires peer approvals before dangerous or critical commands execute.
Stores request state, pending approvals, and execution logs in project files.
Provides session, review, approval, rollback, history, and TUI commands.
Integrates with Claude Code hooks to gate tool execution.
Supports daemon, file polling, webhooks, and event streaming workflows.
limitations: It does not remove the need for least-privilege credentials or backups.
It depends on correct pattern classification and reviewer judgment.
It executes approved commands in the local shell environment.
The marketplace listing should be reviewed before publishing because install and execution paths are high risk.

use_cases.title: Guard Multi-Agent Development
use_cases.description: Require a second agent or reviewer before risky cleanup, reset, or deployment commands run.

use_cases.title: Review DevOps Changes
use_cases.description: Gate Terraform, Kubernetes, database, and cloud commands with approvals and audit records.

use_cases.title: Audit Agent Operations
use_cases.description: Inspect command history, approval decisions, execution outcomes, and rollback context after agent activity.

prompt_templates.title: Start Protected Session
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me initialize SLB for this project and start a session for my current coding agent.

prompt_templates.title: Request Risky Command Approval
prompt_templates.prompt: Use SLB to request approval for this risky command, and include a clear reason for reviewers.

prompt_templates.title: Review Pending Requests
prompt_templates.prompt: Show pending SLB requests, explain the command risk, and recommend approve or reject with reasons.

prompt_templates.title: Design Team Policy
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an SLB policy for AI agents that covers approvals, timeouts, webhooks, rollback capture, and emergency override rules.

output_examples.input: Review a pending dangerous cleanup command.
output_examples.output: A reviewer summary that explains the command target, risk tier, expected effect, rollback options, and an approve or reject recommendation.

output_examples.input: Prepare an SLB policy for Kubernetes deletion commands.
output_examples.output: A concise policy covering required approvals, reviewer separation, timeout behavior, audit logging, and emergency override limits.

output_examples.input: Summarize recent SLB activity for a security review.
output_examples.output: A plain-language audit summary listing high-risk requests, reviewers, outcomes, failed executions, and follow-up actions.

best_practices: Review installation scripts before running remote installers.
Use least-privilege credentials because approved commands inherit the local environment.
Require different reviewers for critical commands and keep rollback capture enabled.
anti_patterns: Do not use emergency execution as a normal approval path.
Do not expose the daemon on a network without authentication and restricted access.
Do not auto-approve commands that modify production infrastructure or data.

faq.question: What does SLB do?
faq.answer: SLB adds a two-person approval workflow before AI agents run risky shell commands.

faq.question: Does SLB execute commands on a remote server?
faq.answer: No. The documentation says commands execute in the user shell environment.

faq.question: Why is this listing high risk?
faq.answer: It includes remote installation, local command execution, inherited credentials, hook writes, webhooks, and optional daemon networking.

faq.question: Can it prevent every destructive action?
faq.answer: No. It adds review gates, but users still need backups, access controls, and least-privilege credentials.

faq.question: Does it support Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents hook installation and generated Claude hook configuration.

faq.question: Should teams publish it without review?
faq.answer: No. A human should review the implementation and installer before marketplace publication.
